elements.sent-2411-001: A perfect $H$-tiling in a graph $G$ is a collection of vertex-disjoint copies of a graph $H$ in $G$ that covers all vertices of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Motivated by papers of Bush and Zhao and of Balogh, Treglown, and Wagner, we determine the threshold for the existence of a perfect $K_{h,h}$-tiling of a randomly perturbed bipartite graph with linear minimum degree.
elements.sent-2411-003: Embedding problems form a central part of both extremal and random graph theory.
elements.sent-2411-004: Many results in extremal graph theory concern minimum degree conditions that guarantee the existence of some spanning subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-005: A celebrated result of Corr\'{a di and Hajnal gives the minimum degree of finding a perfect $K_3$-tiling \cite{corradi1963maximal .}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Later, Hajnal and Szemer\'{e di generalized this result to cliques of arbitrary size~\cite{hajnal1970proof .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Since then, there have been generalizations to the multipartite setting, for instance \cite{zhao2009bipartite, magyar2002tripartite, martin2008quadripartite, keevash2015multipartite .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Recall that the Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi random graph $G_{n,p}$ consists of the vertex set $[n]$ where each edge is present, independently, with probability $p$.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: In this regime, a key question is to establish the threshold for which $G_{n,p}$ contains a spanning subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-010: An $H$-tiling of a graph $G$ is a subgraph consisting of vertex disjoint copies of $H$ and a perfect $H$-tiling of $G$ is an $H$-tiling which spans all vertices of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-011: In their groundbreaking paper, Johansson, Kahn, and Vu~\cite{JKV settled the threshold for which $G_{n,p}$ admits a perfect $H$-tiling for a fixed nonempty, strictly balanced, graph $H$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: More recently, Gerke and McDowell \cite{GerkeMcDowell determined the corresponding threshold when $H$ is what they call nonvertex-balanced graph.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Bohman, Frieze, and Martin~\cite{bohman2003many introduced the randomly perturbed graph model which connects the two questions together.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In the randomly perturbed setting, Balogh, Treglown, and Wagner \cite{balogh2019tilings determined a probability $p$ for the appearance of a perfect $H$-tiling in a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree at least $\alpha n$ and showed that this is best possible for $\alpha<1/|V(H)|$~\cite[Section 2.1]{balogh2019tilings , where $H$ is strictly balanced.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this paper, we let $h\geq 1$ be a positive integer, set $H=K_{h,h}$, and consider tiling the randomly perturbed bipartite graph which consists of two graphs on the same vertex set $A\sqcup B$, $|A|=|B|=n$, and $h \mid n$.
elements.sent-2411-016: One is an arbitrary graph $G_{n;\alpha}$, the other is a random graph $G_{n,n,p}$, each of which is bipartite, and each respects the same bipartition $(A,B)$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Hence each of $A$ and $B$ will always be independent sets.
elements.sent-2411-018: A bipartite graph in which each class is the same size is called balanced.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this setting, $G_{n;\alpha}$ is an arbitrary bipartite graph as above with minimum degree $\delta(G) \geq \alpha n$ and $G_{n,n,p}$ is an Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi random bipartite graph as above, respecting the same bipartition as $G_{n;\alpha}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Note that that we differ from Balogh, Treglown, and Wagner~\cite{balogh2019tilings in that we restrict the deterministic graph, $G_{n;\alpha}$, to be bipartite but we also restrict the appearance of the random edges to appear only between the two vertex disjoint classes.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Zhao~\cite{zhao2009bipartite proved that, for $h\geq 2$, if a balanced bipartite graph on $2n$ vertices, $n$ divisible by $h$, has minimum degree \begin{equation}\delta(G) \geq  \frac{n}{2} +h -1, n/h even \frac{n+3h}{2}-2, n/h odd, \end{equation} and $n$ sufficiently large, then it has a perfect $K_{h,h}$-tiling with no random edges necessary.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Later, the case of tiling by unbalanced complete bipartite graphs was settled by Hladk\'y and Schacht~\cite{Hladkytiling and by Czygrinow and DeBiasio~\cite{Czygrinowtiling .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Bush and Zhao~\cite{Bush later covered the remaining cases.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Note that for any bipartite graph $H$ on $h$ vertices, it suffices to tile a graph with copies of $K_{h,h}$ in order to obtain an $H$-tiling.
elements.sent-2411-025: This is because $K_{h,h}$ itself can be tiled with two copies of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Although weaker conditions could suffice for a perfect $H$-tiling, where $H$ is any bipartite graph, we direct only our attention to the the case of when $H=K_{h,h}$.
elements.sent-2411-027: When $h$ does not divide $n$, then it is not possible to tile all of the vertices of $G_{n;\alpha}$ by copies of $K_{h,h}$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Since our result is asymptotic, if $n$ is not divisible by $h$, then we can delete at most $h-1$ vertices from each part and prove the theorem for the resulting graph.
elements.sent-2411-029: Since the value of $n$ differs by at most a constant, the threshold function will not change.
elements.sent-2411-030: In sum, we will always assume that $n$ is divisible by $h$.
elements.sent-2411-031: We use $\prob(A)$ to denote the probability of event $A$ and use $\E[X]$ to denote the expectation of random variable $X$.
elements.sent-2411-032: We say that a sequence of events $A_1,A_2,\ldots,A_n,\ldots$ occurs with high probability ($\whp$) if $\lim_{n\to \infty \prob (A_n) = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In the perturbed graph setting, we define the threshold as follows: The function $t(n): \mathbb{N \to (0,1)$ is a threshold function for property $P$ if there exist real positive constants $c, C$ such that \begin{equation}p(n) \leq ct(n) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad \exists G_{n;\alpha}^{*} such that G_{n;\alpha}^{*}\cup G_{n,n,p}\not\in P, \whp. p(n) \geq C t(n) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad \forall G_{n;\alpha} we have G_{n;\alpha }\cup G_{n,n,p}\in P, \whp.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}p(n) \leq ct(n) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad \exists G_{n;\alpha}^{*} such that G_{n;\alpha}^{*}\cup G_{n,n,p}\not\in P, \whp. p(n) \geq C t(n) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad \forall G_{n;\alpha} we have G_{n;\alpha }\cup G_{n,n,p}\in P, \whp.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-034: The question of interest is to determine the threshold function for a perfect $K_{h,h}$-tiling in $G_{n;\alpha \cup G_{n,n,p}$, given that $h \mid n$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Bollob\'as and Thomason~\cite{BTthresholds established that such a threshold exists.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Our main result is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-037: For each real number $\alpha \in \bigl(0,1/(2h)\bigr)$, and positive integer $h \geq 1$, a threshold function for the existence of a perfect $K_{h,h}$-tiling is $n^{-(2h-1)/h^2}$.
elements.sent-2411-038: The statement of Theorem~\ref{thm:threshold is proved in two parts.}
elements.sent-2411-039: These are Theorem~\ref{thm: example and Theorem~\ref{thm: main below.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: For each real number $\alpha \in \bigl(0,1/(2h)\bigr)$ and positive integer $h\geq 1$, there exists a constant $c>0$ and a sequence of graphs $\bigl\{G_{n;\alpha ^{*}\bigr\}_{n\in h\mathbb{N }$ for which $G_{n;\alpha ^{*}$ is a balanced bipartite graph on $2n$ vertices, with minimum degree $\delta\bigl(G_{n;\alpha ^{*}\bigr) \geq \alpha n$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The sequence $\bigl\{G_{n;\alpha ^{*}\bigr\}$ satisfies the condition that if $p\leq cn^{-(2h-1)/h^2}$ then, \whp, $G_{n;\alpha ^{*}\cup G_{n,n,p}$ does not contain a perfect $K_{h,h}$-tiling.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: For each real number $\alpha >0$ and positive integer $h\geq 1$, there exists a constant $C>0$ such that any sequence of graphs $\bigl\{G_{n;\alpha}\bigr\}_{n\in h\mathbb{N }$ for which $G_{n;\alpha}$ is a balanced bipartite graph on $2n$ vertices, with minimum degree $\delta\bigl(G_{n;\alpha}\bigr) \geq \alpha n$, the sequence $\bigl\{G_{n;\alpha}\bigr\}$ satisfies the condition that if $p\geq Cn^{-(2h-1)/h^2}$ then, \whp, $G_{n;\alpha}\cup G_{n,n,p}$ contains a perfect $K_{h,h}$-tiling.}
elements.sent-2411-043: As a consequence of Remark~\ref{remark:divisibility , the threshold function for the existence of a $K_{h,h}$-tiling of size at least $\lfloor n/h\rfloor$ in a randomly perturbed balanced bipartite graph, without the divisibility restriction is $n^{-(2h-1)/h^2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: In the event that we begin with an empty graph (i.e. $\alpha =0$), we note that a multiplicative polylog factor is required (Theorem~\ref{thm: covering ).}
elements.sent-2411-045: The phenomenon of removing the polylog factor in the randomly perturbed setting when $\alpha$ is small is found throughout the literature.
elements.sent-2411-046: For example, it occurs in the tiling problem for general (i.e., not bipartite) randomly perturbed graphs \cite{balogh2019tilings, bottcher2023triangles , as well as the case of finding Hamiltonian cycles \cite{bohman2003many , and spanning trees \cite{krivelevich2017bounded , and even in the hypergraph setting \cite{krivelevich2016cycles .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-269: In this paper we have determined the probability threshold for the existence of a perfect $K_{h,h}$-tiling in the randomly perturbed bipartite graph when $0 <\alpha < 1/(2h)$ for all $h\geq1$.

elements.sent-2411-275: We conjecture that this probability threshold coincides with Theorem 1.3 of~\cite{balogh2019tilings .. \bibliographystyle{abbrv.}}
elements.sent-2411-276: We will prove that if $\alpha>0$ and if $p\geq C n^{-1}$, then $G_{n;\alpha}\cup G_{n,n,p}$ contains a perfect matching whp.
elements.sent-2411-277: This is the special case of Theorem~\ref{thm: main for $h=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-278: We verify that the strong Hall condition is met by demonstrating that whp there does not exists a set of vertices $S\subset A$ for which $|S| = |N(S)|+1$.
elements.sent-2411-279: Note that by our assumption on $G_{n;\alpha}$ we must only consider sets of size at least $\alpha n$.
elements.sent-2411-280: For a subset $S \subset A$, let $X_S$ be the indicator variable for the event that $|S|>|N(S)|$.
elements.sent-2411-283: This is because the minimum degree condition guarantees that if $S'\subseteq A$ has size greater than $n-\alpha n$ then $N(S') = B$ by the pigeonhole principle.
elements.sent-2411-284: By Markov's inequality, \begin{equation}\Pr(X \geq 1) \leq \E[X] = \sum \E[X_i] \leq \sum_{\ell=\alpha n+1}^{n-\alpha n} \binom{n}{\ell}\sum_{j=\alpha n}^{\ell-1}\binom{n}{j}(1-p)^{\ell(n-j)} \leq (2^n)^2 \sum_{\ell=\alpha n +1}^{n-\alpha n}(1-p)^{\ell n} \sum_{j=\alpha n}^{\ell - 1} (1-p)^{-\ell j} \leq 2^{2n} \sum_{\ell=\alpha n +1}^{n-\alpha n}(1-p)^{\ell n} \cdot n (1-p)^{-\ell^2} \leq n^2 2^{2n} (1-p)^{n^2/4} \leq \exp\bigl\{2\ln n + 2n\ln 2 -p n^2/4\bigr\}\end{equation} So in fact, $C> 8 \ln 2$ suffices.
elements.formula-2411-025: \begin{equation}\Pr(X \geq 1) \leq \E[X] = \sum \E[X_i] \leq \sum_{\ell=\alpha n+1}^{n-\alpha n} \binom{n}{\ell}\sum_{j=\alpha n}^{\ell-1}\binom{n}{j}(1-p)^{\ell(n-j)} \leq (2^n)^2 \sum_{\ell=\alpha n +1}^{n-\alpha n}(1-p)^{\ell n} \sum_{j=\alpha n}^{\ell - 1} (1-p)^{-\ell j} \leq 2^{2n} \sum_{\ell=\alpha n +1}^{n-\alpha n}(1-p)^{\ell n} \cdot n (1-p)^{-\ell^2} \leq n^2 2^{2n} (1-p)^{n^2/4} \leq \exp\bigl\{2\ln n + 2n\ln 2 -p n^2/4\bigr\}\end{equation}
